disclaimer: Preliminary / tracking. This dashboard describes the current state of the Portuguese economy by combining official high-frequency indicators; it largely co-moves with (and is partly built from) the same activity data published by the Banco de Portugal (notably the Coincident Activity index) and Eurostat. It is a faster, consolidated READ-OUT of that state, NOT a more-accurate forecast of GDP. No tile is statistically shown to beat its same-frequency baseline; treat every number as indicative. The q/q-GDP nowcast in 'official_quarterly' is preliminary and noisy and is only meaningful at quarter-end (M3). All backtests are PSEUDO-real-time (the final revised panel masked by nominal publication lag, NOT true data vintages), so live performance is likely somewhat optimistic; and the effective sample is tiny (≈40 ex-COVID quarters; ~3 recession episodes), so no edge here is statistically significant — this is an honest read-out, not a validated forecasting system. The annual outlook is an arithmetic carryover plus an AR(1) extrapolation that assumes no new shock; its backtested intervals missed in every crisis year.

narrative.en: The Portuguese economy is steady (health score 51/100). Activity is running at about +1.8% year-on-year on the BdP coincident indicator, with the weekly pulse pointing down since that print. Our indicative mid-quarter estimate for Q2 2026 GDP is +0.3% quarter-on-quarter (80% band -0.1% to +1.1%), implying about +1.9% year-on-year. For 2026 as a whole, the median projection is +1.8% annual growth (P(>2%) ≈ 35%); the 5 official forecasts sit between the 46th and 65th percentiles of our distribution. Recession risk is low (P≈9%); in an adverse quarter (5% worst case) GDP growth could come in around -0.7%. The labour market looks stable, with the unemployment rate at 5.7%. Indicative read-out, not an official forecast — see each tile's caveats.
narrative.pt: A economia portuguesa está estável (índice de saúde 51/100). A atividade cresce cerca de +1,8% em termos homólogos segundo o indicador coincidente do BdP, com o pulso semanal a apontar para baixo desde essa publicação. A nossa estimativa indicativa a meio do trimestre para o PIB do 2.º trimestre de 2026 é +0,3% em cadeia (banda de 80%: -0,1% a +1,1%), o que implica cerca de +1,9% homólogos. Para o conjunto de 2026, a projeção mediana é +1,8% de crescimento anual (P(>2%) ≈ 35%); as 5 previsões oficiais situam-se entre os percentis 46 e 65 da nossa distribuição. O risco de recessão é baixo (P≈9%); num trimestre adverso (pior cenário de 5%), o crescimento do PIB rondaria -0,7%. O mercado de trabalho parece estável, com a taxa de desemprego em 5,7%. Leitura indicativa, não uma previsão oficial — ver as ressalvas de cada painel.
narrative.generated_by: fixed-rule template over the published tiles (no model, no new claim)

health_score.label: state of the economy
health_score.honesty_note: A PRESENTATION composite, not a model and not a skill claim. It is a FIXED, transparent monotone blend of three signals the dashboard already shows — the activity pulse (65%), the next-quarter downside (35%), capped by the recession probability — using documented anchor curves, with NO fitting on the score itself. By construction it cannot be more accurate than its parts; its only job is to consolidate them into one honest read. The anchors are tied to PT GDP history (a GFC/Troika/COVID-class contraction maps near 0; trend growth ~50; a boom ~90-100). Inherits every caveat of its inputs: pulse tracks (does not beat) BdP coincident activity, the recession gauge is coincident (timeliness, not anticipation), and the downside band is indicative on a short sample.

source_tiles.pulse_source: anchored combined_read (BdP Coincident Activity + weekly tilt)

pulse.label: preliminary

anchor_honesty.anchor_claim: corr 0.81 ex-COVID with YoY GDP (pseudo-real-time, 40 normal quarters 2014-2025; the monthly BdP 'Coincident Activity' print carries the level information, not the weekly pulse)
anchor_honesty.tilt_claim: direction-only, adds no verified level information (partial corr +0.01); its value is timeliness between monthly BdP prints
anchor_honesty.hybrid_validation: pseudo-real-time check (scripts/validate_pulse.py --hybrid, 48 quarters 2014-2025, ex-2020/21): anchor alone corr 0.83 / RMSE 1.05pp vs YoY GDP; anchor+tilt corr 0.59 / RMSE 1.71pp; raw pulse 0.23 / 1.92pp. Adding the tilt WORSENS the anchor-only level read — treat combined_read as a freshness overlay, and the anchor as the best level estimate

pulse.honesty_note: ANCHORED activity read, not a forecast. The LEVEL is the monthly BdP 'Coincident Activity' print — pseudo-real-time corr 0.81 with realized YoY GDP on normal quarters (40 quarters 2014-2025, ex-2020/21). The weekly index adds NO verified level information beyond that free monthly print (partial corr +0.01; its raw level tracks YoY GDP at only 0.23 ex-COVID): its one asset is CADENCE, so it enters only as the TILT — the direction of the weekly pulse since the BdP print. Measured warning (validate_pulse.py --hybrid, 48 expanding refits ex-2020/21): anchor alone corr 0.83 / RMSE 1.05pp; anchor+tilt 0.59 / 1.71pp; raw pulse 0.23 / 1.92pp — adding the tilt WORSENS the level read, so headline the ANCHOR as the level and read combined_read as a freshness overlay only. ~80% of the weekly index's movement is forward-filled monthly data ('weekly' is cadence, not resolution). Pseudo-real-time masks the final revised panel (not true vintages), so live skill is likely optimistic.

weekly_index.description: Weekly coincident activity index (WEI/Lewis-Mertens-Stock spirit), scaled to YoY GDP growth. Tracking/preliminary.

contributions.summary: preliminary GDP nowcast for 2026Q2 is +0.22% q/q (prior +0.22%); biggest movers: Activity Indicators dragging down (-0.04pp); Surveys pushing up (+0.03pp).
contributions.note: Attribution of the DFM factor nowcast (the repo's factor-news reference model), not the production headline and not a causal claim. Units are q/q GDP growth (decimal).

recession_probability.honesty_note: Coincident recession-risk READ-OUT (current state), NOT a recession forecast. Brier skill +0.43 vs the climatological base rate — BUT it does NOT beat lag-1 persistence (-0.30): since recession labels are autocorrelated, 'same as last quarter' (which a real-time user has) is the honest baseline. Its genuine value is TIMELINESS (a calibrated read available before the GDP print), not prediction skill. AUC 0.91 is ~matched by a single coincident-activity rule, so it mostly consolidates/calibrates activity it co-moves with. Scored on only ~3 OOS episodes (GFC/Troika/COVID); leave-one-recession-out shows the skill leans on Troika (fragile), and it is NOT statistically significant (DM vs base-rate p~0.35). Pseudo-real-time on revised data, so live performance is likely optimistic.

leading_recession_probability.method: expanding-window logistic, 2q-ahead, on leading signals: consumer/industry expectations + financial stress (lagged)
leading_recession_probability.honesty_note: LEADING recession gauge: probability of recession 2 quarters ahead from survey/credit/financial-stress signals. It beats lag-2 persistence out-of-sample (BSS_vs_persistence +0.32; ex-COVID ~+0.23; leave-one-recession-out +0.23..+0.32, not dependent on any single episode; bootstrap P(BSS>0)~0.93) — modest anticipation value. READ THIS BEFORE TRUSTING THE NUMBER — the 'beats persistence' claim is BASELINE-DEPENDENT: the bar here is y_{t-h} (the label h quarters back, matched to the model's signal vintage). Against the TIGHTEST bar, lag-1 persistence ('same as the last published quarter'), this model LOSES (BSS ~-0.36, ex-COVID ~-0.55). And the lag-h win is LARGELY a forecast-horizon artifact, not unique leading skill: a purely COINCIDENT logistic run h-ahead also beats lag-2/lag-3 persistence (any decent recession reading beats a 2-3-quarter-stale label); the leading signals only edge the coincident model. Other caveats: (1) NOT statistically significant on DM (p~0.19) — n≈4 recession episodes is the binding limit; (2) it cannot anticipate sudden shocks — it predicts the 2020 crash from the pre-pandemic 2019Q4 survey and (correctly) misses it; its edge is gradual onsets (GFC-deepening, Troika-onset) and recovery timing; (3) pseudo-real-time on revised survey data, so live skill is likely optimistic. Bottom line: a reasonable, leak-free anticipation gauge, but its headline skill vs persistence is an artifact of the horizon-matched (stale) baseline — do NOT present it as 'beats same-as-last-quarter'.

sahm_tripwire.honesty_note: PT-recalibrated Sahm rule (threshold = 1 std of the PT unemployment-gap distribution, not the US 0.5pp). Caught 3/4 historical PT recessions with 5 false-positive episodes; threshold is in-sample, not out-of-sample optimized.

growth_at_risk.label: risk context, not a forecast
growth_at_risk.honesty_note: Risk CONTEXT, not a point forecast. Conditional quantiles of next-quarter GDP growth (Adrian-Boyarchenko-Giannone style), fitted as a conditional Johnson-SU (location+scale respond to stress+activity; skew/tail shape estimated once). The genuine, leak-free, COVID-robust signal is the DOWNSIDE band (q05/q10): through the production backtest rel-pinball 0.75 vs a GARCH/EWMA baseline ex-COVID (0.81 dropping the 2021 rebound too) — a modest improvement over the earlier independent quantile-regression (0.78/0.84, ~3.5% lower pinball) that holds on BOTH panels. The bigger, more robust win is CALIBRATION: tail hit-rates q05 0.050 / q10 0.075 (vs target 0.05/0.10) where QR overshot the 10% tail at 0.11, and PIT-KS p improves 0.13->0.36. The pinball gain ALONE is not bootstrap-significant (p(JSU<QR)≈0.79, 90% CI on the paired difference includes 0); it is adopted because it is consistently better-calibrated with a small pinball gain and no downside, not because it is a proven skill jump. HONEST attribution (ablation): the downside edge over the baseline comes almost entirely from the real-ACTIVITY regressor, NOT from financial stress (activity-only ~0.78 ≈ full; stress-only ~0.91). Financial stress is a correctly-signed conditioner (higher stress lowers q05 via the scale channel) but carries little of the skill — do not bill this as 'financial-conditions-driven'. Still a short sample (~40 ex-COVID-and-rebound quarters); treat it as indicative, not a validated forecasting claim. Lead with the downside; do not read the median as authoritative. Interval coverage (5-95) is ~0.83 vs 0.90 nominal — the band is still modestly too narrow. Pseudo-real-time on revised data, so OOS skill is likely optimistic.

official_quarterly.label: indicative

implied_yoy.note: Implied YoY = nowcast q/q log-growth plus the three published prior quarters (treated as known); the band width equals the q/q band.

official_quarterly.honesty_note: DEMOTED tile. q/q-GDP is near-white-noise and hard to nowcast; no model here is statistically distinguishable from an AR(1) at the 5% level. Mid-quarter read from shrink_combo (supply-side bridge + mixed-frequency factor DFM + a 3-parameter ESI-momentum equation, inverse-MSE weighted with a hard AR(1) floor): rel-RMSE 0.81 vs AR(1) ex-COVID (corr +0.67, DM p=0.014), bootstrap CI excluding parity under every seed tested, and a direction-consistent replication on a never-touched 2000-2004 window. PROVISIONAL: this configuration was picked as the best of ~19 tested variants; after accounting for that search the evidence it beats our previous combination is suggestive (selection-adjusted p ~ 0.06-0.1), the out-of-window edge is smaller than the headline (~0.01-0.04 rel), and the gain is concentrated in crisis quarters (2009, 2021) — in calm years expect it to roughly match the previous model. We are tracking it on new quarters. An indicative mid-quarter signal, not an authoritative number. The band is the EXPANDING split-conformal interval on this horizon's production model's own realized errors (COVID/2021-rebound excluded), replacing the native model band. It is ASYMMETRIC — wider above the point than below (-0.40pp/+0.73pp at 80%) — because the model's historical errors are skewed: it has tended to under-predict growth. On the normal-quarter backtest it covers 82%/86% at nominal 80%/95%. Full distributional (PIT) calibration also passes on that panel. Coverage is calibrated for normal quarters; a genuine boom/bust will pierce it.
official_quarterly.first_release_note: INE first prints have revised UP by +0.15pp on average (sd 0.34pp, n=36 ex-COVID genuine first releases) relative to today's revised series; our backtest edge survives first-release scoring (M3 rel-RMSE 0.81 vs the production AR(1) — see output/track_record.json).

annual_outlook.honesty_note: Carryover from published quarters plus an AR(1) extrapolation does most of the work; the nowcast narrows the 80% band by ~0.3pp. Backtested intervals miss in crisis years (2008, 2011-12, 2020-21): this outlook assumes no new shock. Pseudo-real-time on revised data; annual-average-of-quarterly may differ slightly from official annual accounts.
annual_outlook.label: annual outlook

forecasts.publication: Relatório de progresso do plano orçamental de médio prazo (abril 2026)

consensus.note: Hand-curated, sourced institutional forecasts (data/consensus_forecasts.csv; latest publication per institution). The percentile places each point forecast on OUR simulated distribution — proximity is context, not validation.

track_record.label: track record
track_record.framing: Until 2026-06-04 these are backtested as-if calls on a leak-free publication-lag harness, scored against the GDP number as FIRST published — from then on every dot is a call we actually published.

backtest.label: backtest (as-if calls, leak-free publication-lag harness)
backtest.definition_genuine: quarters whose first ALFRED vintage is <= 120 days after quarter end; 44 pre-2016 pseudo-first-release quarters (first vintage = 2016-04 backfill snapshot) excluded
notes: M1/M2 rows are shrink_combo (the production in-quarter model); ar1 is the production AR(1) fitted on the SAME publication-lag information set.

hit80 compares |our_call - first_release| to the per-horizon 80% split-conformal half-width from src/evaluation/quarterly_band_calibration.json; those bands were calibrated on overlapping quarters (against the revised target), so the hit-rate is indicative, not an out-of-sample coverage claim.

live.label: 4 days of live history as of 2026-06-11

summary.note: Sparse by construction — the live era only started on 2026-06-04. No live-covered quarter has an official outturn yet; live scoring begins when INE/Eurostat publish the first one.

track_record.honesty_note: Backtest rows are as-if calls from the leak-free publication-lag harness, scored against the GDP number as FIRST published — genuine first releases only (first ALFRED vintage within 120 days of quarter end; pre-2016 pseudo-vintages excluded). Pseudo-real-time (final revised panel masked by nominal lag, not true input vintages), so live performance is likely somewhat worse. Band hit-rates are indicative: the bands were calibrated on overlapping quarters against the REVISED target. The live era is days old and has no scored outturn yet — judge it only as it accumulates.

labour.label: labour market
labour.verdict_rule: fixed sign rule: improving if BOTH the unemployment-rate 3m change (dead-zone ±0.1pp) and the IEFP registered-unemployment 3m change (dead-zone ±2000 persons) are falling; deteriorating if both rising; else stable
labour.honesty_note: A state read-out of published labour data; no forecast — next-month unemployment is at its noise floor (discovery 2026-06-11). The verdict is a fixed sign rule on published 3-month changes, not a model.
